paco.title: PACO Webinar
paco.subtitle: Adaptation Priorities in Central and West Africa
paco.description: Join us for a webinar dedicated to climate change adaptation priorities in Central and West Africa. This event will bring together experts, decision-makers and practitioners to discuss the most urgent adaptation strategies for the region.

presentation.title: Local Authorities Facing Climate Change
presentation.subtitle: Governance, Planning and Financing for Adaptation
presentation.context: In French-speaking countries, decentralized authorities play a strategic role in the concrete implementation of climate change adaptation policies. Located closest to the populations, they are responsible for local planning, land use management and coordination of essential services. However, despite their central position, local authorities face several challenges: limited financial resources, insufficient technical capacities, poor access to climate data and lack of integration of local knowledge into territorial plans.
presentation.objectiveGeneral: Strengthen the capacities of local authorities to plan, finance and implement climate change adaptation actions.

objectives.0: Present the roles, responsibilities and levers of action of local authorities in climate adaptation
objectives.1: Highlight successful experiences in territorial adaptation planning and financing
objectives.2: Identify needs for capacity building, financing and local governance tools
objectives.3: Present innovative approaches to financing adaptation at the local level

0.title: Local Governance
0.desc: Contribution of local authorities to the implementation of NDCs, NAPs and sectoral policies

1.title: Territorial Planning
1.desc: Territorial diagnostics, climate risk management and resilient infrastructure

2.title: Local Financing
2.desc: Access to international funds, green budgets, local taxation and public-private partnerships

3.title: Case Studies
3.desc: Experiences of rural and urban municipalities: water management, resilient agriculture, nature-based solutions

ayi-nkamgna.role: Mayor of Dzeng and President of REMCESS

session2.title: Climate justice and social inclusion in adaptation
session2.subtitle: Climate Webinars – Societies and Communities
session2.context:
Climate change exacerbates social and territorial inequalities, disproportionately affecting vulnerable communities, women, youth, rural populations and marginalized groups. Adaptation can only be effective and sustainable if it integrates the principles of climate justice and social inclusion, ensuring that the benefits of policies and financing actually reach those who need them most.

In the French-speaking world, many local and regional initiatives demonstrate that integrating human rights, equity and inclusive participation strengthens collective resilience. This webinar is part of the initiative « Strengthening the capacities and collaboration of French-speaking countries on climate change adaptation and access to financing », implemented by the Institute of the Francophonie for Sustainable Development (IFDD) with the support of the regional programme « Implementation of Regional and National Adaptation Priorities in Central and West Africa (PACO) », a programme funded by the German Federal Ministry for the Environment, Climate Action, Nature Conservation and Nuclear Safety (BMUKN) under the « International Climate Initiative (IKI) » and implemented by the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Internationale Zusammenarbeit (GIZ) GmbH. It aims to create a space for dialogue on the challenges of climate justice and social inclusion in adaptation strategies.
session2.objectiveGeneral: Promote an inclusive and equitable approach to climate change adaptation, highlighting climate justice as a lever for resilience and social cohesion.

objectives.0: Highlight social and territorial inequalities linked to climate impacts.
objectives.1: Present initiatives integrating equity, gender and inclusion in adaptation projects.
objectives.2: Foster dialogue between institutions, civil society and vulnerable communities on climate justice.
objectives.3: Identify financing and governance mechanisms that promote social inclusion.
objectives.4: Encourage the creation of Francophone networks to convey a common voice on climate justice.

0.title: Climate Justice and Human Rights
0.desc: Principles, issues and integration into public policies.

1.title: Social Inclusion in Adaptation
1.desc: Gender, youth, rural and marginalized populations.

2.title: Case Studies
2.desc: Local and regional initiatives promoting equity and citizen participation.

3.title: Financing and Partnerships
3.desc: Mechanisms to support inclusive and socially just projects.

4.title: Multi-stakeholder Dialogue
4.desc: Synergies between institutions, local authorities, CSOs and communities.

targets.0: Civil society organizations and citizen movements

session3.title: Public health and adaptation to climate impacts
session3.subtitle: Heatwaves, vector-borne diseases and prevention systems
session3.context:
The impacts of climate change on public health are today one of the most pressing challenges for French-speaking countries, particularly in Central and West Africa. The increased frequency and intensity of heatwaves, the resurgence of vector-borne diseases (malaria, dengue, chikungunya, yellow fever), the deterioration of air quality, as well as the increased risks of malnutrition and waterborne diseases, disproportionately affect vulnerable populations.

As previous concept notes recall, local communities, youth, territorial authorities and marginalized groups are often the most exposed to climate effects. Likewise, the health systems of many French-speaking countries face structural constraints: limited resources, insufficiently resilient infrastructure, lack of climate data integrated into alert systems, and weak intersectoral coordination.

This webinar is part of the initiative « Strengthening the capacities and collaboration of French-speaking countries on climate change adaptation and access to financing », implemented by the Institute of the Francophonie for Sustainable Development (IFDD) with the support of the regional PACO programme – funded by BMUKN under the International Climate Initiative (IKI) and implemented by GIZ. It aims to create a space for exchange and mobilization around public health issues linked to climate, focusing on preventive approaches, alert systems, health governance and community-based solutions.
session3.objectiveGeneral: Strengthen the capacities of public health stakeholders, local authorities and community organizations to anticipate, prevent and manage the health impacts of climate change, particularly heatwaves and vector-borne diseases.

objectives.0: Present the main health risks linked to heatwaves, vector-borne diseases and environmental changes.
objectives.1: Highlight innovative initiatives in epidemiological surveillance, early warning and community-based prevention.
objectives.2: Foster dialogue between health institutions, climatologists, local authorities and civil society organizations.
objectives.3: Identify needs for capacity building, financing and decision-support tools for climate health.
objectives.4: Encourage regional collaboration and the dissemination of good practices in health adaptation.

0.title: Heatwaves and public health
0.desc: Physiological impacts, risk groups, prevention plans, alert systems and crisis communication.

1.title: Vector-borne diseases and climate
1.desc: Evolution of risk areas, entomological surveillance, community prevention strategies, technological innovations.

2.title: Resilient health systems
2.desc: Adapted infrastructure, climate emergency management, integration of climate data into health information systems.

3.title: Community approaches and inclusion
3.desc: Role of youth, women, community health workers and local authorities in prevention.

4.title: Financing health adaptation
4.desc: National and international opportunities, multi-stakeholder partnerships, integration of health into national adaptation plans.

targets.0: Public health and epidemiological surveillance professionals
targets.2: Civil society organizations and citizen movements

surveillance-epi.role: Physician, epidemiologist and founder of Dawa Mobile Health, researcher at the Julius Global Health Centre, Utrecht University (Netherlands).

communautaire.role: Community actor or young leader engaged in health prevention

session4.title: Challenges and use of data for climate adaptation
session4.subtitle: Science, research and innovation for adaptation
session4.context:
Climate adaptation increasingly depends on the availability, quality and strategic use of data. Yet, in many French-speaking countries, climate information systems remain fragmented, incomplete or difficult to access. Data from meteorology, hydrology, remote sensing, socio-economic surveys or local knowledge are often underused, due to a lack of technical capacity, suitable tools or effective sharing mechanisms.

The challenges are multiple: insufficient observation infrastructure, lack of interoperability between institutions, low recognition of local data, difficulties in accessing open data, and absence of clear governance frameworks. However, when data are mobilized consistently, they help improve planning, anticipate risks, target vulnerable populations and strengthen the relevance of adaptation projects.

This webinar is part of the initiative « Strengthening the capacities and collaboration of French-speaking countries on climate change adaptation and access to financing », implemented by the Institute of the Francophonie for Sustainable Development (IFDD) with the support of the regional PACO programme – funded by BMUKN under the International Climate Initiative (IKI) and implemented by GIZ. It aims to create a space for exchange on issues related to climate data and their use for effective, inclusive and evidence-based adaptation.
session4.objectiveGeneral: Strengthen the understanding and capacities of French-speaking stakeholders on challenges, opportunities and good practices related to the production, management and use of data for climate adaptation.

objectives.0: Highlight the main challenges related to the availability, quality and accessibility of climate and socio-economic data.
objectives.1: Present innovative initiatives in data collection, analysis and visualization for adaptation.
objectives.2: Foster dialogue between public institutions, research centres, community organizations and technical partners on data governance.
objectives.3: Identify tools, platforms and approaches to improve data-driven decision-making.

0.title: Structural challenges of climate data systems
0.desc: Observation infrastructure, gaps, institutional fragmentation, data access.

1.title: Data for adaptation planning
1.desc: Vulnerability analysis, risk mapping, climate scenarios, socio-economic data.

2.title: Digital tools and platforms
2.desc: Geographic information systems (GIS), remote sensing, open data, decision dashboards.

3.title: Valorisation of local data and community knowledge
3.desc: Integration of citizen observations, participatory data, endogenous knowledge.

4.title: Governance and partnerships
4.desc: Data sharing, interoperability, institutional frameworks, collaboration between stakeholders.

5.title: Case studies
5.desc: Successful experiences in French-speaking countries, technical innovations, pilot projects.

targets.6: Specialized media and science communication actors

noblet.role: Expert in climate vulnerability and adaptation, 10 years of experience in climate change adaptation

el-khatri.role: Mission officer to the Director General of the National Meteorology of Morocco, national coordinator of the RCE programme

session5.title: Youth and Climate Change
session5.subtitle: Education and citizen awareness for adaptation
session5.context:
In French-speaking African countries, young people represent a major share of the population and play a decisive role in transforming societies. They remain among the groups most exposed to the effects of climate change, due to their high socio-economic vulnerability and limited access to training, cooperation and funding opportunities.

Faced with accelerating climate risks such as droughts, floods, land degradation and food insecurity, strengthening the capacities of youth becomes an essential lever to support adaptation, encourage local innovation and foster sustainable and inclusive solutions. Climate education, citizen awareness and community engagement are fundamental pillars to strengthen resilience and promote active youth participation in decision-making processes at various levels.

This webinar is part of the initiative « Strengthening the capacities and collaboration of French-speaking countries on climate change adaptation and access to financing », implemented by IFDD with the support of the regional PACO programme – funded by BMUKN under the International Climate Initiative (IKI) and implemented by GIZ.
session5.objectiveGeneral: Strengthen the knowledge, skills and engagement of youth and community stakeholders on climate change adaptation, with a focus on education, citizen awareness and youth-led local initiatives.

objectives.0: Present the main challenges and opportunities related to youth involvement in climate change adaptation.
objectives.1: Highlight innovative awareness, climate education and citizen mobilization initiatives in French-speaking African countries.
objectives.2: Foster the sharing of experiences among young leaders, civil society organizations, educational institutions and local authorities.
objectives.3: Encourage the development of networks and regional collaborations to strengthen climate action led by Francophone youth.

0.title: Climate education as a resilience tool
0.desc: Integration of adaptation into educational curricula, pedagogical tools, participatory approaches.

1.title: Youth citizen mobilization
1.desc: Awareness campaigns, community actions, climate leadership, role of media and social networks.

2.title: Youth-led local initiatives
2.desc: Sustainable agriculture, integrated water management, nature-based solutions, social innovation, green economy, gender.

3.title: Funding and support
3.desc: Climate financing opportunities for youth-led projects.

targets.0: Young leaders, associations and youth movements
targets.4: Public institutions involved in climate adaptation
targets.5: Media, influencers and citizen communication actors

itsopo-ngolet.role: General Coordinator, Climathon Gabon 2025, JVE Gabon / Killimo Ecologia

daleba.role: Consultant in social and climate justice, JVE Côte d'Ivoire / African Climate Justice Collective

session6.title: Indigenous communities and endogenous knowledge
session6.subtitle: Towards equitable adaptation strategies
session6.context:
Indigenous and local communities hold endogenous knowledge that has enabled them, for generations, to sustainably manage natural resources, preserve ecosystems and adapt to environmental variations. This knowledge, rooted in fine observation of environments and proven practices, constitutes today essential levers to strengthen resilience in the face of growing impacts of climate change.

Yet, in many French-speaking countries, this knowledge remains insufficiently documented, recognized or integrated into adaptation strategies. Socio-cultural transformations, resource pressure, biodiversity loss and the erosion of traditional practices threaten its transmission and valorisation.

This webinar is part of the initiative « Strengthening the capacities and collaboration of French-speaking countries on climate change adaptation and access to financing », implemented by IFDD with the support of the regional PACO programme – funded by BMUKN under the IKI and implemented by GIZ.
session6.objectiveGeneral: Strengthen the understanding, valorisation and integration of endogenous and indigenous knowledge into climate change adaptation strategies, with a focus on lessons learned from local and indigenous experiences.

objectives.0: Present the role of indigenous and local communities in climate change adaptation.
objectives.1: Highlight concrete experiences illustrating the use of endogenous knowledge in different French-speaking contexts.
objectives.2: Identify lessons learned, good practices and success factors observed in the field.
objectives.3: Foster dialogue between researchers, institutions, indigenous organizations and adaptation stakeholders.
objectives.4: Encourage capitalisation and dissemination of knowledge from these experiences.
objectives.5: Encourage the integration of indigenous knowledge into the design of locally and culturally sensitive adaptation projects.

0.title: Endogenous knowledge and climate resilience
0.desc: Traditional land, forest, water and biodiversity management practices, agroecology, intergenerational transmission.

1.title: Lessons learned from local experiences
1.desc: Case studies from Central and West Africa, lessons from pilot projects, success factors and limits.

2.title: Linking traditional and scientific knowledge
2.desc: Dialogue and co-construction mechanisms, documentation, capitalisation and synergies with modern innovations.

3.title: Integration into adaptation strategies
3.desc: Favourable conditions, role of institutions and local authorities, funding opportunities for initiatives based on local knowledge.

targets.0: Organizations and representatives of indigenous communities
targets.4: Conservation and natural resource management actors
targets.5: Donors, NGOs and technical and financial partners
